title: Chapter 3: The Alchemist's Convenience Store
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D4+D12 -->
The gas station outside King City had a name: "KWIK-E-MART." The "K" was flickering, the "W" was missing entirely, and someone had drawn a pentagram in the dust on the front window.
Chloe saw none of this.

What Chloe saw was: an ancient alchemist's guildhall, its sigil half-dimmed by centuries of neglect, its wards flickering with the last remnants of protective magic. The pentagram in the dust was clearly a summoning circle — amateur work, probably a rogue apprentice — but she appreciated the effort.
"We need snacks," Sam said, already halfway out of the car. "And gas. And possibly a new car."
"Shadowfax is fine," Chloe said.
"Shadowfax is making a noise I've never heard before, and I've been driving this car for four years."
"New sounds are just new songs."
Sam's eye twitched. She took a sip of her americano — three seconds, mild disapproval — and walked toward the store.

Chloe adjusted her crown and followed.
The bell above the door chimed. Chloe classified this as "the Herald Bell of Entry — enchanted to announce visitors to the guildmaster."

Inside: fluorescent lights that buzzed like dying incantations. Rows of colorful packages that Chloe recognized as "alchemical potions and enchanted rations." A slushie machine that was clearly "a portal to the Elemental Plane of Sugar."

The man behind the counter was approximately sixty years old, wearing a faded NASCAR shirt, and had the expression of someone who had seen everything and was not impressed by any of it. Chloe classified him as "the Gatekeeper."
"Morning," the Gatekeeper said.
"Well met, Gatekeeper," Chloe replied.
The man blinked slowly. "We have a special on Monster Energy. Two for four dollars."
"Ah — a bargain on Potions of Vigor. The guild is generous."
Another slow blink. Then the man looked at Sam, as if to ask: *Is your friend okay?*
Sam's expression said: *I have been asking that question for three years and I still don't have an answer.*

Chloe wandered the aisles like she was exploring an ancient library. Doritos: "Crystalline sustenance wafers." Beef jerky: "Dried rations of the northern barbarian tribes." The rotating hot dog machine: "The Eternal Flame of Sustenance — a sacred artifact."
She stopped in front of the refrigerated section. The glass door was fogged with condensation. Behind it: rows of energy drinks glowing in neon colors.
"Sam," Chloe breathed. "Look."
"It's Monster Energy."
"No. These are the Elixirs of the Seven Schools of Magic."
She pointed. "Green — the Elixir of Vitality. White — the Elixir of Clarity. The pink one — that's the Elixir of Charm, very dangerous, only advanced alchemists should handle it."
"The pink one is strawberry-flavored," Sam said.
"Same thing."
Sam grabbed two Monster Energy cans — green for Chloe, white for herself — and a bag of Doritos that Chloe had identified as "the Nacho Cheese Crystals of the Ancients." She approached the counter.
That's when the door chimed again.

Two men walked in. Plaid shirts. Trucker hats. One of them was already mid-conversation, and the volume of his voice suggested he believed everyone within a fifty-foot radius was deeply interested in his opinions.
"...and I told him, I said, you can't just let these people come in here and take our jobs, you know? It's basic economics."
Chloe's spine straightened.
"Sam," she whispered.
"Don't," Sam whispered back.
"They're Lesser Trolls. Hate-speech variety. I can sense their aura."
"They're just guys in trucker hats."
"Same thing."
"Chloe. We're in a gas station in King City. We have twelve dollars in cash and a car that might not start. Do not start a fight with the locals."
Chloe considered this. Then she adjusted her crown.
"I'm not going to start a fight," she said. "I'm going to perform an exorcism."
"Chloe—"

But Chloe was already walking toward the two men, her cloak billowing behind her (the cloak was actually a poncho she'd bought at a rest stop, but the effect was similar). She stopped three feet from them, planted her feet, and raised her hand — not in greeting, but in the gesture of a wizard about to cast a spell.
"Gentlemen," she said, her voice dropping into her "court wizard" register.
Both men turned. The louder one — red-faced, probably mid-forties — squinted at her. "The hell are you supposed to be?"

"I am Artemis, Queen of Avalon, and I have sensed a darkness clinging to your words. A curse, if you will — a curse of small-mindedness, passed down through generations, feeding on fear and ignorance."
The man's mouth opened. Closed. Opened again. His friend started laughing.
"Is this one of those performance art things?" the friend asked.
"This is an intervention," Chloe said. "The curse can be broken. But you must perform the Ritual of Self-Reflection."
"The what?"

Back in the car, Sam sat in the driver's seat for a full thirty seconds without starting the engine. She stared at the steering wheel. She stared at the KWIK-E-MART sign. She stared at the two men through the window — the red-faced one was now standing in the parking lot, looking at the grass like he was actually considering touching it.
"You called him a troll," Sam said finally.
"He was a troll."
"To his face."
"Where else would I call him?"
Sam's hands were shaking slightly. Chloe noticed — Chloe always noticed. She reached over and placed her hand on top of Sam's, pressing it gently against the steering wheel.
"You okay?"
"I thought he was going to hit you."
"He wasn't going to hit me."
"How do you know?"
"Because trolls don't hit. They bluster. They shout. They're cowards. That's the whole point of being a troll — you're too afraid to actually fight, so you just make noise and hope people back down."
Sam considered this. "That's... actually a really good analysis of toxic masculinity."
"Thank you. I learned it from a D&D sourcebook."
Sam almost laughed. The laugh got stuck again, somewhere around her ribs this time, but Chloe saw it. Chloe always saw it.
"You're insane," Sam said.
"You said that already."
"It bears repeating."
Chloe grinned. She put her crown back on — it had slipped during the exorcism — and buckled her seatbelt. "Onward, noble steed. The road awaits."
Sam turned the key. The engine coughed twice, shuddered, and came to life — almost eagerly this time, as if Shadowfax too had enjoyed the gas station battle.
"Where to?" Sam asked.
"South," Chloe said. "The scrolls tell me there's a great pink windmill ahead — a structure of immense magical power, built by an ancient civilization of vain sorcerers."
Sam pulled up her phone. "That's the Instagram-famous strawberry farm with the giant pink windmill. It's two hundred miles south."
"Exactly — the pink windmill of the vain sorcerers."
"Same thing," Sam muttered, and pulled onto the highway.

Sam glanced in the rearview mirror. The gas station was shrinking behind them, the KWIK-E-MART sign a faint neon glow against the brown hills. The two trucker-hat men were still standing in the parking lot. One of them was looking at his shoes. The other one — Dave, the troll — was looking at the grass.

"Chloe?"
"Yeah?"
"I think you might actually be magic."
Chloe adjusted her crown. "I know."
